title: Praxis Special Education: Severe to Profound (5547) - 200-Item Diagnostic Bank
description: Two 100-item diagnostics, an exact-blueprint 120-item timed simulation, detailed option feedback, and native learning support for K-12 educators serving learners with severe or profound disabilities.
contentReview: 200 source-reviewed items; independent severe-disabilities educator, AAC, assistive-technology, health, family, accessibility, legal, cultural-linguistic, and psychometric review pending
simulationLabel: 120-question Special Education Severe to Profound simulation
disclaimer: Independent preparation material, not affiliated with or endorsed by ETS or CEC. Results are not official forms, scaled scores, pass predictions, licenses, diagnoses, eligibility or placement decisions, medical or legal advice, crisis plans, or substitutes for individualized assessment and current requirements.

domains.label: Human Developmental and Individualized Learning Needs
Planning and Instruction and the Learning Environment
Ethical and Legal Practice, Professionalism, and Collaboration

items.prompt: For a learner with severe or profound disabilities, a kindergarten teacher notices that one child reaches several language milestones later than classmates. What is the most appropriate first interpretation?
choices: Development follows broad patterns, but individual variation requires information from multiple sources and settings before conclusions are drawn.
Any difference from a class average proves a disability.
The child should immediately receive a fixed disability label.
Chronological age no longer matters when planning instruction.
items.rationale: Milestones provide useful reference points, not stand-alone diagnoses. Educators should document functioning across domains, contexts, and time and involve qualified team members before attributing a pattern to disability. For 5547 preparation, preserve dignity and meaningful participation while coordinating communication, health, sensory, motor, behavioral, academic, adaptive, and transition supports; use individualized evidence rather than assumptions based on disability severity.
choiceRationales: Correct. Milestones provide useful reference points, not stand-alone diagnoses. For 5547 preparation, preserve dignity and meaningful participation while coordinating communication, health, sensory, motor, behavioral, academic, adaptive, and transition supports; use individualized evidence rather than assumptions based on disability severity.

items.prompt: For a learner with severe or profound disabilities, a student solves multistep problems during small-group instruction but rarely begins the same work during a noisy whole-class period. What should the teacher examine first?
choices: Assume the student has forgotten the underlying skill.
Compare task demands, environmental conditions, supports, and performance across the two contexts.
Lower every academic expectation for the student.
Treat the difference as deliberate noncompliance.
items.rationale: Performance reflects an interaction among learner skills, task demands, supports, and context. Comparing conditions can identify a removable barrier before the team changes expectations or infers a stable trait. For 5547 preparation, preserve dignity and meaningful participation while coordinating communication, health, sensory, motor, behavioral, academic, adaptive, and transition supports; use individualized evidence rather than assumptions based on disability severity.

items.prompt: For a learner with severe or profound disabilities, a teacher wants to understand a learner’s self-regulation needs. Which information is most useful?
choices: A single global rating completed months ago.
The support profile without current observations.
Observations of triggers, strategies used, recovery, and successful conditions across routines.
The average behavior of the rest of the class.
items.rationale: Self-regulation is best understood through specific, current observations of what precedes difficulty, how the learner responds, which strategies help, and whether patterns differ by setting or task. For 5547 preparation, preserve dignity and meaningful participation while coordinating communication, health, sensory, motor, behavioral, academic, adaptive, and transition supports; use individualized evidence rather than assumptions based on disability severity.

items.prompt: For a learner with severe or profound disabilities, a multilingual learner has difficulty following rapid oral directions in English but demonstrates the task after a peer models it. Which interpretation is best?
choices: The performance automatically establishes an intellectual disability.
Visual modeling should be removed to reveal the true score.
Home-language information is irrelevant to educational planning.
Language access may be affecting the demonstration of knowledge, so the team should gather culturally and linguistically appropriate evidence.
items.rationale: Difference is not disability. The team should distinguish language acquisition and access from disability by using multiple methods, appropriate language supports, and information about performance in the learner’s languages. For 5547 preparation, preserve dignity and meaningful participation while coordinating communication, health, sensory, motor, behavioral, academic, adaptive, and transition supports; use individualized evidence rather than assumptions based on disability severity.

items.prompt: For a learner with severe or profound disabilities, a student with a disability is embarrassed when an adult uses age-inappropriate materials for a functional routine. What principle should guide revision?
choices: Use age-respectful materials while matching the instructional steps and supports to the learner’s current needs.
Match all materials to developmental level regardless of age.
Stop teaching functional skills until grade-level mastery is reached.
Ask peers to complete the routine for the student.
items.rationale: Instruction can be individualized without sacrificing dignity. Materials and contexts should be age-respectful, while prompts, task steps, and response modes are adapted to the learner’s present performance. For 5547 preparation, preserve dignity and meaningful participation while coordinating communication, health, sensory, motor, behavioral, academic, adaptive, and transition supports; use individualized evidence rather than assumptions based on disability severity.

items.prompt: For a learner with severe or profound disabilities, a student performs a social routine only with one familiar adult. Which plan best promotes generalization?
choices: Repeat the skill only in the original setting until it is perfect.
Practice the same skill with planned variations in people, materials, and natural settings, then fade prompts.
Introduce several unrelated target skills at the same time.
Wait for generalization to occur without programming it.
items.rationale: Generalization is more likely when instruction deliberately varies relevant people, materials, and settings, reinforces the skill in natural routines, and gradually transfers stimulus control away from adult prompts. For 5547 preparation, preserve dignity and meaningful participation while coordinating communication, health, sensory, motor, behavioral, academic, adaptive, and transition supports; use individualized evidence rather than assumptions based on disability severity.

items.prompt: For a learner with severe or profound disabilities, a team is writing a learner profile. Which statement is most useful for planning?
choices: The student is requiring intensive support in most situations.
The student has autism and therefore prefers routines.
The student follows a two-step visual routine independently and needs one verbal prompt when the sequence changes.
The student is developmentally much younger than peers.
items.rationale: Objective descriptions identify a demonstrated strength, a specific condition, and the support needed. Global labels and assumptions based on category do not tell the team what to teach or how to measure change. For 5547 preparation, preserve dignity and meaningful participation while coordinating communication, health, sensory, motor, behavioral, academic, adaptive, and transition supports; use individualized evidence rather than assumptions based on disability severity.

items.prompt: For a learner with severe or profound disabilities, a learner’s participation drops after a recent family move and change in transportation. How should the school respond?
choices: Attribute the change entirely to the support profile.
Remove the student from general education immediately.
Ignore nonacademic factors because they are outside instruction.
Consider environmental changes alongside disability-related needs and coordinate supports without blaming the learner or family.
items.rationale: Development and learning are influenced by environmental as well as biological factors. A responsive team gathers context, protects privacy, stabilizes access, and avoids treating a situational change as proof of a new disability need. For 5547 preparation, preserve dignity and meaningful participation while coordinating communication, health, sensory, motor, behavioral, academic, adaptive, and transition supports; use individualized evidence rather than assumptions based on disability severity.

items.prompt: For a learner with severe or profound disabilities, a teacher records one difficult morning and says a student has lost a previously learned skill. What evidence is needed before changing the program?
choices: Collect repeated observations and work samples under representative conditions and compare them with prior data.
Make the change because the most recent event is always most valid.
Use the disability label as the only source of evidence.
Wait until the annual review and gather no additional data.
items.rationale: A single observation may be affected by health, context, fatigue, or task features. Repeated measures and comparison with baseline information support a more reliable decision about whether performance truly changed. For 5547 preparation, preserve dignity and meaningful participation while coordinating communication, health, sensory, motor, behavioral, academic, adaptive, and transition supports; use individualized evidence rather than assumptions based on disability severity.

items.prompt: For a learner with severe or profound disabilities, a student with a specific learning disability reads grade-level text accurately but cannot organize written paragraphs. Which response is best?
choices: Provide identical remediation in all academic areas.
Target the documented area of academic need instead of assuming the disability affects every subject in the same way.
Infer low overall intelligence from the one difficulty.
Replace instruction with permanent adult completion.
items.rationale: Specific learning disabilities can affect particular psychological processes and academic skills unevenly. Instruction should be based on the learner’s actual pattern of strengths and needs, not a global assumption. For 5547 preparation, preserve dignity and meaningful participation while coordinating communication, health, sensory, motor, behavioral, academic, adaptive, and transition supports; use individualized evidence rather than assumptions based on disability severity.

items.prompt: For a learner with severe or profound disabilities, a student with autism covers their ears during fire-drill announcements and then misses directions. Which support is most responsive?
choices: Interpret the response as intentional defiance.
Exclude the student from every future drill.
Plan a predictable, accessible warning and communication routine while teaching the student how to use it.
Require exposure without preparation until the response stops.
items.rationale: Sensory and communication needs can affect access and behavior. A predictable warning, accessible direction, and taught coping or communication response support participation while preserving safety requirements. For 5547 preparation, preserve dignity and meaningful participation while coordinating communication, health, sensory, motor, behavioral, academic, adaptive, and transition supports; use individualized evidence rather than assumptions based on disability severity.

items.prompt: For a learner with severe or profound disabilities, a student with ADHD begins work when directions are chunked and a checklist is visible. What does this pattern suggest?
choices: The student knew the work and was choosing to fail before.
The checklist has changed the academic standard.
The disability affects intelligence rather than self-management.
Executive-function supports are improving task initiation, organization, and monitoring.
items.rationale: ADHD may affect executive functions such as initiation, working memory, inhibition, and organization. External structures can support performance without changing the learning target or implying weak ability. items.prompt: For a learner with severe or profound disabilities, a student with an emotional or behavioral disability argues mainly during unpredictable peer-group tasks. What perspective is most useful?
choices: Examine interactions among the learner, task, peers, expectations, and consequences rather than locating the problem only within the child.
Assume the behavior will occur equally in every setting.
Conclude that peers alone caused the disability.
Remove all social learning opportunities permanently.
items.rationale: An ecological perspective considers how behavior emerges through interactions between the learner and the environment. Context-specific data can guide prevention, instruction, and reinforcement of more effective responses. For 5547 preparation, preserve dignity and meaningful participation while coordinating communication, health, sensory, motor, behavioral, academic, adaptive, and transition supports; use individualized evidence rather than assumptions based on disability severity.

items.prompt: For a learner with severe or profound disabilities, which lesson design best reflects Universal Design for Learning?

items.prompt: When planning intensive, age-respectful support, an educator learns of a possible abuse or safety concern. Which response is professionally appropriate?
choices: Investigate independently before telling anyone.
Follow mandatory reporting and school safety procedures promptly, share only with authorized personnel, and do not promise confidentiality that cannot be kept.
Promise secrecy to preserve rapport.
Discuss the concern with unrelated colleagues.

items.prompt: When planning intensive, age-respectful support, an outside employment provider may contribute to transition planning. What should the school address before inviting the representative?
choices: Invite any outside provider without notice because transition is discussed.
Transfer all school responsibilities to the agency.
Obtain the required parent or adult-student consent before inviting an outside agency likely to provide or pay for transition services.
Wait until after graduation to coordinate services.
items.rationale: Transition collaboration can improve continuity, but outside-agency participation must follow consent and confidentiality requirements. The public agency retains its IDEA responsibilities even when another agency is involved. items.prompt: When planning intensive, age-respectful support, a candidate learns the federal IDEA transition age but will practice in a state with additional rules. What is the correct professional habit?
choices: Local policy may remove federal rights.
One preparation textbook permanently replaces current law.
Requirements are identical in every jurisdiction and never change.
Federal law establishes a baseline, while state law, licensure rules, and local policy may add timelines, procedures, or protections.
items.rationale: Competent practice checks current authoritative requirements. State and local rules can provide additional protections or earlier timelines but cannot reduce controlling federal rights; this study tool is not legal advice.
